summary: I was working at the Statue of Liberty on September 11, 2001, when the attacks happened. At first, when I heard a plane hit one of the towers, I thought it was just some stunt because we often see crazy things around here in New York City. The island became a place of chaos and support as we helped injured people and tried to keep everyone calm. It was a place where all the employees came together to help each other and the emergency responders during that terrible day.

Living and working in New York City, I was used to seeing all kinds of unusual things, so when the first plane hit the tower, I initially thought it was just a stunt. But as the events unfolded on September 11, 2001, it became clear this was a serious terrorist attack. The city was in shock, with bridges and highways shut down, and everyone was trying to get home or help in any way they could. New York City showed both its vulnerability and its incredible spirit of togetherness that day.

My husband worked at Trinity Church, which is just a few blocks from the World Trade Center. On September 11, 2001, I was worried about him because the attacks happened so close to where he worked. I remember talking to my coworker Delores about my concern, and then the first tower came down, which made the situation even more frightening. Trinity Church was part of the area deeply affected by the events that day.

The World Trade Center was at the center of the tragedy on September 11, 2001. I was on the Statue of Liberty when the first plane hit one of the towers, and then the second plane hit, causing chaos and confusion. I saw the towers come down from the island, and it was a terrifying and surreal moment. The area around the Trade Center was shut down, and many of my coworkers and emergency personnel were involved in rescue and relief efforts.

On September 11, 2001, I was working at the Statue of Liberty when the attacks began. At first, we thought the first plane hitting the tower was some kind of stunt, but soon it became clear it was a terrorist attack. The island became a triage center for injured people, and I helped by organizing names and providing water and support. The day was filled with shock, fear, and a strong sense of community as everyone came together to help each other and the emergency responders.

I live on Staten Island, and on September 11, 2001, getting home was a big challenge because all the bridges were closed. I stayed at the Statue of Liberty until late that night helping feed emergency workers and coworkers. Eventually, a police officer gave me a ride home with a police escort since the usual routes were blocked. Staten Island felt far away that day, but I was grateful to get home safely after such a long and difficult day.

Cynthia Garrett was the Assistant Superintendent at the Statue of Liberty on September 11, 2001. She was the one who told us to meet outside by the flags after the second plane hit and helped organize the staff during the chaos. Cynthia was involved in coordinating the response and ensuring everyone was accounted for and safe. She was a calming presence amid the confusion and helped manage the evacuation efforts.

Superintendent Dayson was in charge during the September 11 attacks at the Statue of Liberty. She made the decision to send non-essential employees home to keep them safe, though I chose to stay and help. Dayson was focused on the safety of the staff and the operation of the island during the crisis. She later got a police escort home along with other key staff members.

Walt Fleming was a member of the maintenance staff at the Statue of Liberty on September 11, 2001. During the chaos, he came to inform us that we might be going back inside to get our belongings or possibly sent home. His role was part of the support staff helping manage the situation on the island. Walt was one of the people helping to keep things organized amid the confusion.

Delores was our former AO at the Statue of Liberty, and I was talking to her on September 11, 2001, when I expressed my worry about my husband working near the Trade Center. She tried to reassure me that everything would be okay, even as the first tower came down. Delores was a comforting presence during a very frightening time, helping to calm some of the anxiety among the staff.

Linda DaVoe was one of my coworkers at the Statue of Liberty on September 11, 2001. Along with Cathy Aiello and me, she helped prepare food in the concessions office to feed firefighters, EMTs, and others who were working tirelessly on the island. Linda was part of the team effort that brought everyone together to support the emergency personnel during that tragic day.

Cathy Aiello was a coworker who worked alongside Linda DaVoe and me on September 11, 2001, at the Statue of Liberty. We all pitched in to prepare and serve food to the firefighters and EMTs who were exhausted from the rescue efforts. Cathy was part of the spirit of cooperation and helping that defined that day on the island.

Danny Brown was the Chief of Interpretation who lives in Staten Island and was at the Statue of Liberty on September 11, 2001. Like Superintendent Dayson and Cynthia Garrett, he was given a police escort home because the bridges were closed. Danny was part of the staff who had to coordinate their departure from the island under difficult circumstances. I remember staying late while they left with the escort, worried about how I would get home.
